output: TypeScript is a statically typed superset of JavaScript developed by Microsoft. It adds optional type annotations, interfaces, enums, and generics. TypeScript compiles to plain JavaScript. It catches bugs at compile time instead of runtime — a massive advantage for large codebases.

TypeScript catches type errors at compile-time, provides killer IDE autocomplete (IntelliSense), makes refactoring safer, and improves team communication through explicit types. For any codebase over 1000 lines with more than one developer — TypeScript isn't optional, it's mandatory.

Install globally: `npm install -g typescript`. Compile with `tsc file.ts`. For projects: add as devDependency with `npm install -D typescript` and generate config: `tsc --init`. This creates `tsconfig.json` where you control all compiler options.

input: What is an interface in TypeScript?
output: An interface defines the shape/contract of an object — what properties and methods it must have. `interface User { id: number; name: string; email?: string; }` — the `?` marks optional fields. Interfaces are erased at compile-time and have zero runtime cost.

input: What is the difference between type and interface in TypeScript?
output: Both define shapes, but `interface` supports declaration merging (you can add to it later) and is better for objects/classes. `type` is more powerful — it can represent unions (`string | number`), intersections, mapped types, and conditional types. Prefer `interface` for public APIs, `type` for everything complex.

Generics allow you to write reusable, type-safe code without hardcoding types. Example: `function identity<T>(arg: T): T { return arg; }`. This works with ANY type while still being typesafe. Used heavily in utility types like `Array<T>`, `Promise<T>`, and `Pick<T, K>`.

Built-in helpers that transform types: `Partial<T>` (all fields optional), `Required<T>` (all fields required), `Readonly<T>` (immutable), `Pick<T, K>` (subset of fields), `Omit<T, K>` (exclude fields), `Record<K, V>` (key-value map), `ReturnType<F>` (extract function return type). Use them constantly.

Docker is a platform for packaging apps into isolated containers. A container bundles code + runtime + dependencies into one portable unit. It runs identically on any machine — dev laptop, CI server, or AWS production. The definitive solution to 'it works on my machine' syndrome.

A container is a lightweight, isolated process running on the host OS. Unlike VMs, containers share the host kernel — they're much faster and use far less memory. A container is a running instance of a Docker image. You can run dozens of containers on a single machine.

A Dockerfile is a recipe for building a Docker image. Key instructions: `FROM node:20-alpine` (base image), `WORKDIR /app` (set working dir), `COPY package*.json ./` (copy dependency files), `RUN npm ci` (install deps), `COPY . .` (copy code), `EXPOSE 3000`, `CMD ["node", "index.js"]`. Order matters — unchanged layers are cached.

Docker Compose orchestrates multi-container apps via a `docker-compose.yml` file. You define services (app, postgres, redis, nginx), networks, and volumes. `docker-compose up -d` starts everything. `docker-compose down` stops it. Essential for local development environments.

Kubernetes (K8s) is the industry standard for container orchestration in production. It automatically deploys, scales, self-heals, and load-balances containers across a cluster. Core concepts: Pod (1+ containers), Deployment (desired state), Service (networking), Ingress (routing), and ConfigMap (config). Overkill for small apps, essential at scale.

CI (Continuous Integration): automatically run tests + build on every code push — catch bugs in minutes, not weeks. CD (Continuous Deployment): automatically deploy passing builds to staging or production. Tools: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, CircleCI, ArgoCD. Modern engineering standard — if you're not doing CI/CD, you're doing it wrong.

GitHub Actions is a CI/CD platform built into GitHub. Define workflows in `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`. Trigger on `push`, `pull_request`, or `schedule`. Run jobs on Ubuntu/Windows/Mac runners. Common steps: checkout code, setup Node, run tests, build Docker image, push to registry, deploy. Free for public repos.

Nginx is a high-performance web server and reverse proxy. Use cases: serve static files, reverse proxy to Node.js apps, SSL termination, load balancing, rate limiting, and caching. For Node.js: Nginx listens on port 80/443 and forwards requests to your app on port 3000. Much more efficient than Node serving static files directly.

GraphQL is a query language for APIs by Facebook. Unlike REST's fixed endpoints, GraphQL exposes a single endpoint (`/graphql`). Clients send queries specifying exactly what data they need — no more over-fetching or under-fetching. Use `query` to read, `mutation` to write, `subscription` for real-time. The schema is the single source of truth.

input: What is the difference between REST and GraphQL?
output: REST: multiple fixed endpoints, server controls response shape, good HTTP caching, simpler to understand. GraphQL: single endpoint, client controls response shape, eliminates over/under-fetching, strong type system, excellent for complex linked data. Choose REST for simple CRUD APIs; GraphQL for complex, interconnected data with many clients.

WebSocket provides persistent, bidirectional communication between client and server over a single TCP connection. Unlike HTTP (always client-initiated), WebSockets let the server push data anytime. Use cases: chat apps, real-time dashboards, live notifications, multiplayer games, collaborative editing. In Node.js: use `ws` or `socket.io` library.

JWT (JSON Web Token) is a self-contained authentication token with 3 parts: `header.payload.signature` — all Base64-encoded. The signature is cryptographically signed with a secret key, so any modification is detectable. The payload contains claims (user ID, roles, expiry). Stateless: no session storage needed on the server.

Flow: 1. User POSTs credentials. 2. Server validates and returns a signed JWT. 3. Client stores JWT (httpOnly cookie recommended over localStorage). 4. Client sends `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header with each request. 5. Server verifies signature and extracts user data. No DB lookup needed per request — the token IS the session.

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser security feature that blocks requests from a different origin (domain/port/protocol). The browser sends a preflight OPTIONS request; the server must respond with correct `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` headers. In Express: `npm install cors` then `app.use(cors({ origin: 'https://myapp.com' }))`. Never use `*` in production.

input: How to fix CORS error in Node.js?
output: Install: `npm install cors`. In Express: `app.use(cors({ origin: 'http://localhost:3000', credentials: true }))`. In Fastify: `npm install @fastify/cors` then `fastify.register(require('@fastify/cors'), { origin: true })`. For development you can allow all origins; in production restrict to your exact frontend domain.

SQL injection is when an attacker injects malicious SQL code through user input. Classic example: username `' OR '1'='1` bypasses authentication. Prevention: ALWAYS use parameterized queries: `db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [userId])`. Never concatenate user input into SQL. ORMs (Prisma, Sequelize) handle this automatically.

bcrypt is the industry-standard algorithm for hashing passwords. It's deliberately slow (configurable work factor) and includes a salt to prevent rainbow table attacks. Never store plain-text passwords! Usage: `const hash = await bcrypt.hash(password, 12);` // 12 = cost factor. Verify: `await bcrypt.compare(inputPassword, storedHash);`

Redis is an in-memory data structure store — blazing fast (sub-millisecond latency) because it lives in RAM. Supports: strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams. Primary use cases: caching (store DB query results), session storage, rate limiting, pub/sub messaging, job queues, leaderboards. Run it via Docker: `docker run -d -p 6379:6379 redis`.

MongoDB is the most popular NoSQL document database. Stores data as BSON (Binary JSON) documents with flexible schemas — no ALTER TABLE needed. Collections = tables, Documents = rows. Supports powerful aggregation pipelines, full-text search, geospatial queries, and horizontal sharding. Great for content-heavy apps and rapid prototyping.

MySQL is the most widely deployed open-source relational database — powers WordPress, Shopify, Twitter, YouTube. It's fast, battle-tested, and easy to deploy. Fully ACID-compliant (with InnoDB engine). Supports stored procedures, triggers, and full-text search. For new projects consider PostgreSQL — it's strictly more capable, but MySQL is everywhere.

input: MySQL vs PostgreSQL which is better?
output: PostgreSQL (Postgres) wins on features: better standards compliance, native JSON support, window functions, CTEs, table inheritance, advanced indexing types (GIN, GiST, BRIN), PostGIS for geospatial. MySQL is faster for simple read-heavy workloads and has wider hosting support. For new projects: use Postgres. For legacy: whatever's already there.

SQLite is a self-contained, serverless, zero-configuration SQL database engine. The entire database is a single file on disk. No server process to run, no network. Perfect for: mobile apps (Android/iOS default), embedded systems, desktop apps, prototyping, unit testing, and small web apps with low write concurrency. The most widely deployed database in the world.

Use SQLite when: you're building a mobile app, desktop app, small single-user tool, testing environment, or a simple low-traffic website (< 100 concurrent writes). Avoid SQLite for: high-concurrency web apps, apps needing network access from multiple servers. For those use PostgreSQL or MySQL.

An index is a data structure (usually a B-tree) that drastically speeds up SELECT queries at the cost of slower writes and extra disk space. Without an index, the DB scans every row (O(n)). With an index, it's O(log n). Always index columns used in WHERE clauses, JOINs, and ORDER BY. Use `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` to verify index usage.

A transaction groups multiple DB operations into a single atomic unit. ACID properties: **A**tomic (all-or-nothing), **C**onsistent (leaves DB in valid state), **I**solated (concurrent transactions don't interfere), **D**urable (committed data survives crashes). Use transactions when multiple operations must all succeed or all fail — e.g., bank transfers.

input: SQL vs NoSQL which to choose?
output: SQL: structured data, fixed schema, complex joins, ACID transactions, financial/medical apps. NoSQL: flexible schema, horizontal scaling, document/key-value data, high write throughput, user-generated content. Reality: most production apps use both — SQL for core business data, Redis for caching, MongoDB or Elastic for specific features.

PostgreSQL is the most advanced open-source relational database, with 35+ years of development. Features: full ACID, JSON/JSONB columns, CTEs, window functions, table partitioning, logical replication, PostGIS extension. Handles terabytes of data in production. Used by Apple, Uber, Netflix, and Instagram. The default choice for serious backends.

An ORM (Object-Relational Mapper) maps database rows to language objects, letting you query with code instead of SQL. Node.js ORMs: **Prisma** (modern, type-safe, generates TypeScript types from your schema — use this for new projects), **Sequelize** (mature, complex), **TypeORM** (TypeScript-first, similar to Spring JPA), **Knex** (query builder, not full ORM).

Prisma is a next-generation ORM for Node.js and TypeScript. Define your schema in `schema.prisma`, run `prisma migrate dev` to generate migrations, and get a fully typed client. Example: `const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { email: 'test@test.com' } });` — that's type-safe at compile time. Game-changing DX.

React is Facebook's JavaScript library for building reactive UIs from reusable components. Key mental model: `UI = f(state)` — your UI is a pure function of state. When state changes, React re-renders only the affected components via a Virtual DOM diffing algorithm. Hooks (useState, useEffect, useContext) make functional components powerful.

Next.js is the production-ready React framework by Vercel. Adds: file-system routing (`/pages` or `/app`), Server-Side Rendering (SSR), Static Site Generation (SSG), Incremental Static Regeneration, API routes, image optimization, and Edge runtime. The default choice for any serious React application. `npx create-next-app@latest`.

input: What is the difference between SSR and CSR?
output: CSR (Client-Side Rendering): server sends empty HTML, browser executes JS, renders page. Good for dashboards and authenticated apps. Bad for SEO. SSR (Server-Side Rendering): server executes code and sends complete HTML. Fast first paint, great SEO. Next.js lets you choose per-page: `getServerSideProps` (SSR), `getStaticProps` (SSG), or default client rendering.

Vite is a next-gen build tool by Evan You (Vue creator). It exploits native ES Modules — the dev server starts in milliseconds (no bundling!) and only transforms files on demand. Production builds use Rollup. Supports React, Vue, Svelte, and vanilla JS. Run: `npm create vite@latest`. Replace Webpack/CRA for new projects.

Tailwind is a utility-first CSS framework. Instead of semantic class names (`.card`, `.button-primary`), you apply atomic utility classes directly: `<button class="bg-blue-600 hover:bg-blue-700 text-white font-bold py-2 px-4 rounded-lg transition-all">`. JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler purges unused styles — tiny final CSS bundle. Controversial, but enormously productive.

Design patterns are proven, reusable solutions to commonly recurring software design problems. Categorized into 3 groups: **Creational** (how objects are created), **Structural** (how objects are composed), **Behavioral** (how objects communicate). Coined by the 'Gang of Four' (GoF) in their 1994 book. They're a shared vocabulary for developers.

Singleton ensures only ONE instance of a class ever exists and provides a global access point. Use cases: database connection pool, logger, config manager. In Node.js: module caching makes every `require()` return the same object — modules ARE singletons by default! Anti-pattern if overused (hidden global state, hard to test).

Factory pattern creates objects without exposing the creation logic. Instead of `new Dog()` or `new Cat()`, you call `AnimalFactory.create('dog')`. Use when: object creation is complex, you want to decouple creation from usage, or you need to choose implementation at runtime. Common in frameworks and plugin systems.

Observer (Publish-Subscribe) defines a one-to-many dependency: when one object (publisher/subject) changes state, all dependents (observers/subscribers) are notified automatically. Examples: Node.js EventEmitter, DOM event listeners, Redux store subscriptions, WebSocket notifications. Fundamental pattern in every UI framework.

Strategy defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each, and makes them interchangeable. The context delegates the algorithm to a strategy object. Example: sorting with different algorithms (bubble, quick, merge) selected at runtime. In Node.js: Passport.js authentication strategies (local, Google OAuth, JWT) are a perfect real-world example.

Decorator adds new behavior to an object dynamically without modifying its class. Wraps the original object and extends functionality. Classic example: `CoffeeWithMilk(CoffeeWithSugar(SimpleCoffee()))`. TypeScript has first-class `@Decorator` support. Popular in NestJS for `@Controller()`, `@Get()`, `@Injectable()` etc.

Repository abstracts data access behind an interface. Instead of raw SQL or ORM queries scattered in business logic, you have a `UserRepository` with methods like `findById()`, `save()`, `delete()`. Business logic talks only to the repository interface — easy to swap the DB or mock for tests. Core pattern in Domain-Driven Design.

MVC (Model-View-Controller) separates concerns: **Model** (data + business logic), **View** (presentation/UI), **Controller** (handles requests, orchestrates M and V). Traditional server-side MVC: Express routes → Controller → Model/DB → render View. In SPAs, MVC evolved into component-based architecture (React, Vue), but the principle remains.

Middleware is a chain of functions, each processing a request/response and passing control to the next. Express/Fastify are built entirely on this pattern: `app.use((req, res, next) => { /* do something */ next(); })`. Also used in Redux (middleware for actions), Koa (onion model), and Railway-oriented programming. Composable and extensible by design.

input: What is the Dependency Injection pattern?
output: DI is a technique where a class receives its dependencies from outside instead of creating them internally. Instead of `class UserService { db = new Database(); }`, you do `class UserService { constructor(private db: Database) {} }`. The DB instance is 'injected'. Benefits: loose coupling, easy testing (inject mocks), configurable. NestJS's entire architecture is built on DI.

Builder constructs complex objects step-by-step. Instead of a constructor with 10 parameters, you chain methods: `new QueryBuilder().select('*').from('users').where('active = true').limit(10).build()`. Makes complex object construction readable and flexible. Prisma's query API is essentially a fluent Builder pattern.

SOLID is 5 principles for maintainable OO design: **S** – Single Responsibility (one class, one job). **O** – Open/Closed (open for extension, closed for modification). **L** – Liskov Substitution (subclasses must be substitutable for parent). **I** – Interface Segregation (lean interfaces). **D** – Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions, not concretions). Master these and your code quality jumps 10x.

DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself): every piece of knowledge should have a single, authoritative representation in the system. Duplication leads to bugs: fix a bug in one copy, forget the other copy, system breaks. Solution: extract shared logic into functions, modules, or services. But don't over-DRY — sometimes duplication is cheaper than the wrong abstraction.

KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid): systems work best when kept simple. Complexity is the #1 enemy of maintainable software. Before adding abstraction layers, generics, and patterns — make sure you actually need them. Start with the simplest thing that works, refactor when you see the actual patterns emerge. 'Premature optimization is the root of all evil.' — Knuth

async/await is ES2017 syntax that makes asynchronous code look synchronous. Mark function with `async`, use `await` before any Promise. Under the hood it's still Promises — just cleaner syntax. Always use try/catch for error handling. `await` can only be used inside `async` functions (or top-level modules in modern Node.js).

input: What is a Promise in JavaScript?
output: A Promise represents the eventual result of an async operation. States: `pending` → `fulfilled` or `rejected`. API: `.then(onSuccess)`, `.catch(onError)`, `.finally(cleanup)`. Compose with `Promise.all([p1, p2])` (all must succeed), `Promise.allSettled([p1, p2])` (wait for all regardless), `Promise.race([p1, p2])` (first to finish wins).

input: What is the event loop in JavaScript?
output: The event loop makes JavaScript non-blocking despite being single-threaded. It has: Call Stack (runs sync code), Web APIs (browser/Node handles async work), Callback Queue (completed async callbacks wait here), Microtask Queue (Promises go here — higher priority). The loop: run stack to completion → drain microtask queue → take one callback from callback queue → repeat.

A closure is a function that has access to variables from its enclosing scope even after that scope has returned. Example: `function counter() { let n = 0; return () => ++n; }` — the returned function closes over `n`. Used everywhere: callbacks, event handlers, module pattern, memoization, partial application, and React hooks (useState closes over component scope).

input: What is the difference between let const and var?
output: `var`: function-scoped, hoisted (declaration moves to top), can be re-declared, leaked from `if`/`for` blocks. `let`: block-scoped, not hoisted (TDZ), can be reassigned. `const`: block-scoped, not hoisted, cannot be reassigned (but objects/arrays are still mutable). Rule: use `const` always, `let` when you need to reassign, never `var` in modern code.

input: What is prototypal inheritance in JavaScript?
output: In JavaScript, objects inherit directly from other objects via the prototype chain. Every object has a `[[Prototype]]` link to another object. Property lookup: if not found on the object, JS walks up the chain until it hits `null`. `class` syntax is syntactic sugar over prototypes. `Object.create(proto)` gives you explicit prototype control.

Generators are functions that can pause and resume. Defined with `function*`, they use `yield` to return values one at a time. `next()` resumes execution until the next `yield`. Use cases: lazy sequences, infinite iteration, custom iterables, and cooperative multitasking. async/await is actually implemented with generators under the hood.

input: What is event delegation in JavaScript?
output: Instead of attaching event listeners to each child element, attach ONE listener to a parent. The event bubbles up, and you check `event.target` to identify the source. Massive performance benefit when dealing with lists of 100+ items. Example: listen on `<ul>`, handle clicks on any `<li>` inside it. Fundamental DOM optimization technique.

npm (Node Package Manager) is Node.js's package manager and the world's largest software registry (2M+ packages). Key commands: `npm install` (install all deps from package.json), `npm install express` (add dependency), `npm install -D jest` (add devDependency), `npm run test` (run script), `npm publish` (publish your package), `npx` (run packages without installing).

input: What is the difference between npm and yarn?
output: Yarn was Facebook's answer to early npm performance and reliability issues. Both now use lockfiles, parallel installs, and workspaces. Modern npm (v7+) is on par with Yarn Classic. Yarn Berry (v2+) introduced Plug'n'Play (no node_modules!). pnpm is the rising favorite — hard links mean a single copy of each package across all projects, saving gigabytes of disk space.

package.json is the Node.js project manifest. Contains: `name`, `version`, `description`, `main` (entry point), `scripts` (shell commands run via `npm run`), `dependencies` (production), `devDependencies` (dev-only: testing, build tools), `engines` (required Node version), `type: "module"` (enable ESM). Created with `npm init -y`.

Express.js is Node.js's most popular minimal web framework. Routes: `app.get('/users', handler)`, `app.post('/users', handler)`. Middleware chain: `app.use(cors())`, `app.use(express.json())`, `app.use(authMiddleware)`. Minimalist by design — you add exactly what you need via npm packages. Powers millions of APIs but consider Fastify for new high-performance projects.

Middleware is a function `(req, res, next) => void`. It runs in sequence for each request. Call `next()` to pass to the next middleware, `next(error)` to jump to error handlers. Popular middleware: `cors` (headers), `helmet` (security), `morgan` (logging), `express.json()` (body parsing), `express-rate-limit` (throttling), custom auth guards.

Node.js is a JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 engine. It runs JavaScript outside the browser — on servers, CLIs, desktop apps. Uses an event-driven, non-blocking I/O model, so it handles thousands of concurrent connections on a single thread. Created by Ryan Dahl in 2009. npm is its package manager and ecosystem.

Node.js runs on a single thread with a non-blocking event loop. Instead of spawning threads per connection, it registers async callbacks for I/O (network, files, DB) via libuv. When I/O completes, callbacks are queued and processed. This makes Node.js exceptional for I/O-bound tasks (APIs, real-time apps) but poor for CPU-intensive work (use worker_threads for that).

Fastify is a high-performance Node.js web API framework — benchmarks show 30-50% more throughput than Express. Key features: JSON Schema validation built-in (validated and serialized via fast-json-stringify), powerful plugin system with encapsulation, TypeScript support out of the box, and excellent logging via Pino. The future of Node.js APIs.

input: how to create REST API with node js
output: Quick start with Express: `npm init -y && npm install express`. Create `app.js`: `const express = require('express'); const app = express(); app.use(express.json()); app.get('/api/items', (req, res) => res.json({ items: [] })); app.post('/api/items', (req, res) => { const item = req.body; res.status(201).json(item); }); app.listen(3000, () => console.log('API ready'));`

NestJS is a progressive Node.js framework built with TypeScript that brings Angular-like architecture to the backend. Uses decorators: `@Controller()`, `@Get()`, `@Injectable()`. Built-in: Dependency Injection, Modules system, Interceptors, Guards, Pipes. Excellent for large enterprise applications where structure and conventions matter. Built on top of Express or Fastify.

Microservices splits an application into small, independently deployable services — each owning one business domain (Auth Service, Payment Service, Notification Service). Each service has its own database, scales independently, and communicates via HTTP APIs or message queues. Benefits: independent deployment, fault isolation, technology flexibility. Cost: operational complexity, distributed systems challenges.

input: what is the difference between monolith and microservices
output: Monolith: one codebase, one deployment. Simple to develop, debug, and deploy initially. Scales as one unit. Becomes a 'big ball of mud' at scale. Microservices: dozens of small services. Complex infrastructure, distributed tracing, eventual consistency challenges. Scales precisely. Worth the complexity only after 50+ developers or clear domain boundaries. Start monolith, split later.

A message queue (RabbitMQ, Kafka, AWS SQS, BullMQ+Redis) enables async communication between services. Producer publishes a job; consumer processes it when ready. Benefits: decoupling (services don't need to know each other), buffering (handle traffic spikes), guaranteed delivery (persist messages), async processing (email sending, image resizing). Essential in distributed systems.

Caching stores expensive computation results in fast storage for reuse. Levels: L1 in-process cache (simple object), Redis (distributed cache shared across instances), CDN (edge cache for static assets). Key concerns: cache invalidation (when to update cache — the hardest problem in CS), TTL (time-to-live), eviction policy (LRU, LFU), and cache stampede (thundering herd) prevention.

A load balancer distributes incoming traffic across multiple backend servers. Algorithms: Round Robin, Least Connections, IP Hash, Weighted. Enables horizontal scaling (add more servers instead of bigger servers), high availability (if one server dies, traffic reroutes), and zero-downtime deployments (remove old servers, add new ones). Nginx, HAProxy, AWS ALB, Cloudflare are popular choices.

Git is a distributed version control system by Linus Torvalds. Every developer has the full repo history locally. Fundamental workflow: `git pull` (get latest), make changes, `git add .` (stage), `git commit -m 'feat: add login'` (save snapshot), `git push` (upload). Branches allow parallel development. Merge/rebase combines work. Non-negotiable skill for any developer.

input: What is the difference between git merge and git rebase?
output: `merge`: creates a new 'merge commit' combining two branches. Preserves exact history — you see when branches diverged and merged. Safe for shared branches. `rebase`: replays your commits on top of the target branch. Produces linear, clean history. NEVER rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared branch — you rewrite history and force-push = chaos for teammates.

input: How to resolve git merge conflict?
output: Git marks conflicts with: `<<<<<<< HEAD` (your changes), `=======` (separator), `>>>>>>> branch-name` (incoming changes). Steps: 1. Open conflicting file. 2. Choose what to keep (or combine both). 3. Delete all conflict markers. 4. `git add <file>`. 5. `git commit`. VS Code and JetBrains have visual merge editors that make this much easier.

GitFlow: `main` (production), `develop` (integration), `feature/*`, `release/*`, `hotfix/*` branches. Complex but structured. **Trunk-based development**: everyone commits to `main` daily with feature flags. Simpler, integrates CI/CD naturally. GitHub Flow: `main` + short-lived feature branches merged via Pull Requests. Most teams use this. Choose based on team size and release frequency.

Artificial Intelligence is the science of creating systems that can perform tasks that typically require human intelligence: reasoning, learning, perception, language understanding, and problem-solving. Modern AI is dominated by Machine Learning — systems that learn from data rather than being explicitly programmed. AI is now embedded in every software product you use.

Machine Learning is a subset of AI where models learn patterns from data automatically. Types: **Supervised** (labeled training data, predict output), **Unsupervised** (find hidden patterns in unlabeled data), **Reinforcement** (agent learns by reward/punishment), **Self-supervised** (GPT-style, predicts masked parts of input). Your node-nlp uses supervised classification.

A neural network is a computational graph of layers of neurons. Input layer receives features, hidden layers learn representations, output layer produces predictions. Learning = adjusting weights via backpropagation (gradient descent: move weights in the direction that reduces error). Deep Learning = many hidden layers. brain.js (inside node-nlp) implements a neural net for intent classification.

input: What is NLP natural language processing?
output: NLP is AI applied to human language. Pipeline: Tokenization → Stop-word removal → Stemming/Lemmatization → Feature extraction (TF-IDF, embeddings) → Classification. Tasks: intent recognition, NER, sentiment analysis, machine translation, summarization, question answering. Node-nlp handles the classic NLP pipeline. Transformers (BERT, GPT) handle the deep learning version.

input: What is LLM large language model?
output: LLMs (GPT-4, Gemini, Claude, LLaMA) are transformer-based neural networks trained on trillions of tokens. They learn to predict the next token — but at scale, this emerges into reasoning, coding, writing, and problem-solving. They have billions of parameters. Cost: millions of dollars to train. You interact with them via APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google). The LLM era fundamentally changed software.

input: What is RAG retrieval augmented generation?
output: RAG combines an LLM with a vector database. Instead of relying only on the LLM's training data, you: 1. Convert your docs to embeddings (vector representations). 2. Store in vector DB (Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector). 3. At query time, find semantically similar chunks. 4. Inject them into the LLM prompt as context. Result: LLM answers from YOUR data, not just training data.

Embeddings are dense vector representations of text (or images, audio). Semantically similar content has vectors that are close in high-dimensional space. Example: 'king' - 'man' + 'woman' ≈ 'queen'. Used for: semantic search, recommendation systems, RAG, clustering related content. Models: OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small`, Sentence Transformers locally.

Sentiment analysis classifies text by emotional tone: positive, negative, or neutral. Use cases: monitor brand mentions on social media, analyze customer reviews, detect negative feedback in support tickets, gauge user satisfaction. node-nlp includes sentiment analysis — call `manager.getSentiment('en', text)` and it returns a score from -1 (negative) to +1 (positive).

In AI/LLMs, a token is the basic unit of text. NOT always a word — it can be a word piece, punctuation, or subword. 'hello' = 1 token. 'ChatGPT' = 2 tokens. 'unbelievable' might be 3-4 tokens. Why tokens matter: LLMs have a context window measured in tokens (GPT-4: 128k). API pricing is per token. The LLM predicts the NEXT token — that's its entire mechanism.

input: what is the best programming language?
output: The correct answer is: the one that solves your problem most efficiently. But objectively: JavaScript/TypeScript for web (frontend + backend), Python for AI/data, Rust for systems, Go for DevOps tooling. The worst answer is 'PHP' — but even PHP pays well, so who am I to judge. Pick the right tool for the job.

input: how to become a good developer?
output: 1. Build things. Real things. Break them. Fix them. 2. Read code — open-source projects teach more than tutorials. 3. Learn fundamentals: data structures, algorithms, networking, OS basics. 4. Master version control (Git). 5. Write tests. Future you will be grateful. 6. Learn to Google efficiently — the senior dev skill nobody admits to. 7. Never stop being curious.

Stop reading tutorials passively — BUILD projects. Pick one language (JavaScript/Python are great starts), one small project idea, and ship it. When you hit a wall (you will), that wall becomes your lesson. Real learning is 20% reading and 80% 'why the hell isn't this working'. Embrace the frustration — that's the job.

Technical debt is the accumulated cost of cutting corners in code. Fast hacks today = expensive rewrites tomorrow. Types: deliberate (knowingly taking shortcuts with a plan to fix later) and inadvertent (didn't know better at the time). Track it, communicate it to stakeholders, and regularly allocate time to pay it down. Like financial debt: small amounts are fine, uncontrolled debt = bankruptcy.

The scientific method applied to code: 1. Reproduce the bug reliably. 2. Form a hypothesis of the cause. 3. Add logs/breakpoints around the suspected area. 4. Isolate the minimal reproduction. 5. Fix and verify. Tools: console.log (underrated), Node.js debugger, VS Code debugger, Chrome DevTools. The #1 rule: never assume, always verify.

Code review is the practice of having teammates read and critique code before it merges. Benefits: catch bugs before production, share knowledge, enforce code style, improve design. Good reviewer: comments on 'why' not just 'what', suggests not demands, looks for logic bugs over style nits. Use automated linting to handle style — humans for logic and architecture.
